aliases.name: Zetetic Astronomy
aliases.meaning: the 19th-century system of Samuel Rowbotham, from Greek zetein 'to seek'

description.summary: Flat Earth is a modern conspiracy belief that the Earth is a flat plane (typically a disc with the Arctic at the centre and Antarctica as a perimeter ice wall) rather than the oblate spheroid established by more than two millennia of observational and physical evidence.
description.full: The Flat Earth movement is a modern conspiracy theory community that rejects the spherical Earth model in favour of one of several flat-Earth cosmologies — most often a disc with the Arctic at the centre, an ice wall (interpreted as Antarctica) along the edge, and a dome enclosing a relatively small sun and moon. The movement traces its modern lineage to the Englishman Samuel Rowbotham (1816–1884), whose 1838 'Bedford Level' experiments and 1865 book Earth Not a Globe established 'Zetetic Astronomy'. Lady Elizabeth Blount's Universal Zetetic Society (founded 1893) carried the tradition into the 20th century; Samuel Shenton revived it in 1956 as the International Flat Earth Research Society, which Charles K. Johnson led from Lancaster, California, between 1971 and 2001. The contemporary movement re-emerged after 2009, when Daniel Shenton resurrected the Flat Earth Society as a web-based community, and grew rapidly between 2014 and 2019, propelled by YouTube algorithm dynamics and figures such as Mark Sargent, Mike Hughes, and rapper B.o.B. The 2018 documentary Behind the Curve (dir. Daniel J. Clark) followed several leading believers and notably recorded a 2017 ring-laser-gyroscope experiment in which their own apparatus measured the Earth's rotation. Although flat-Earth claims have been definitively refuted by everything from Eratosthenes' c. 240 BCE shadow measurement to satellite imagery, the modern movement persists as a sociological and online phenomenon and is the most-studied case in the contemporary literature on conspiracy belief.

etymology.origin: The English phrase 'flat earth' is descriptive. The term Zetetic, used by 19th-century proponents, derives from Greek zētēin, 'to seek', and was adopted by Samuel Rowbotham as the name of his investigative method.
etymology.meaning: 'Flat Earth' simply asserts that the Earth's surface is a plane rather than a curved spheroid. 'Zetetic' frames the project as one of empirical seeking opposed to deductive Newtonian science.
etymology.first_use: 1849 (Rowbotham's pamphlet Zetetic Astronomy, A Description of Several Experiments); 1865 (Rowbotham's Earth Not a Globe).

related_phenomena.name: Hollow Earth
related_phenomena.description: Alternative pseudoscientific cosmology popular in the 19th century

features.description: The most common modern model: a flat disc with the Arctic at its centre and Antarctica reimagined as a circumferential ice wall containing the oceans
Many believers posit a solid dome (the biblical 'firmament') enclosing the disc and supporting a small, near sun and moon
Holds that NASA and other space agencies have falsified all space imagery and astronaut testimony

physical.morphology: Flat-Earth belief is a community of overlapping models rather than a single coherent cosmology; researchers (Landrum & Olshansky 2019, Olshansky 2020) typically distinguish faith-based, conspiracy-based, and experiential factions.

habitat_preference: online video and social-media platforms (YouTube, TikTok, Telegram)
annual Flat Earth International Conferences (FEIC) in the United States and Europe
patterns: Sharp post-2014 growth correlated with YouTube recommendation algorithms
Strong overlap with other conspiracy communities (NASA hoax, moon-landing denial)
Recurring use of empirical-looking experiments (Bedford Level, ring-laser gyro, Antarctic 'final experiment') whose results are reinterpreted to preserve the flat-Earth model

range.description: The modern Flat Earth movement is a global, primarily English-speaking online community with concentrations in the United States, the United Kingdom, Brazil, and parts of Africa and South Asia, and with significant enclaves in Russian and Spanish-speaking communities.

hotspots.name: Bedford Level (Old Bedford River)

location.description: Six-mile straight artificial drainage channel in the Cambridgeshire Fens used by Samuel Rowbotham for his 1838 'flat-earth' experiment

hotspots.notes: Site of Rowbotham's foundational experiment and of Alfred Russel Wallace's 1870 wager-funded refutation.

location.description: Headquarters of Charles K. Johnson's International Flat Earth Research Society of America from 1972 onwards

first_recorded.source: Samuel Rowbotham's Bedford Level experiment (1838); Earth Not a Globe (1865)

peak_activity_periods.description: Rowbotham's 'Zetetic Astronomy' lecture circuit and writing in Britain
Samuel Shenton and Charles K. Johnson lead the International Flat Earth Research Society
YouTube-driven mass revival of flat-Earth belief; first Flat Earth International Conference held in 2017 (Cary, North Carolina)

origins.cultural_roots: The modern flat-Earth movement is not a survival of pre-modern cosmology — educated Europeans had accepted a spherical Earth since classical antiquity — but a 19th-century English Protestant biblical-literalist reaction against Newtonian astronomy.
origins.earliest_accounts: Samuel Rowbotham's 1838 Bedford Level experiment and his 1849 pamphlet Zetetic Astronomy: A Description of Several Experiments, expanded as Earth Not a Globe (1865), are the foundational texts of the modern movement.
origins.folklore_connections: The medieval-Earth-was-flat narrative is itself a 19th-century myth (Russell, 1991; Inventing the Flat Earth) and is unrelated to the actual Christian and Islamic learned tradition of the Middle Ages.

timeline.event: Samuel Rowbotham conducts his Bedford Level experiment, claiming to demonstrate a flat Earth
Samuel Rowbotham publishes Earth Not a Globe
Naturalist John Hampden's wager prompts Alfred Russel Wallace to repeat the Bedford Level experiment, definitively demonstrating Earth's curvature
timeline.significance: First systematic scientific refutation of Rowbotham's claims
timeline.source: Garwood, C. (2007), Flat Earth: The History of an Infamous Idea

timeline.event: Lady Elizabeth Blount founds the Universal Zetetic Society
Samuel Shenton founds the International Flat Earth Research Society in Dover, England
timeline.significance: Modern revival of organised flat-Earth belief

timeline.event: Charles K. Johnson assumes leadership of the International Flat Earth Research Society of America in Lancaster, California
timeline.significance: Society peaks at c. 3,500 members under his leadership

timeline.event: Daniel Shenton officially relaunches the Flat Earth Society as a web-based forum
timeline.significance: Beginning of the modern online revival

timeline.event: First Flat Earth International Conference held in Cary, North Carolina
timeline.significance: First major in-person convention of the YouTube-era movement

timeline.event: Mike Hughes launches a homemade steam rocket in California in pursuit of flat-Earth observation
timeline.significance: Highly publicised event of the YouTube era; Hughes died in a 2020 launch attempt

timeline.event: Behind the Curve documentary released (dir. Daniel J. Clark)
timeline.significance: Most influential documentary on the modern movement; includes the ring-laser-gyroscope rotation experiment

timeline.event: YouTube announces algorithmic changes to reduce promotion of conspiracy content, citing flat-Earth belief
timeline.significance: Platform-level intervention in the spread of flat-Earth content

timeline.event: 'The Final Experiment' Antarctic trip takes flat-Earth and globe-Earth representatives to observe the 24-hour Antarctic sun
timeline.significance: High-profile direct empirical test (organised by Will Duffy); most participants accepted the spherical-Earth conclusion, though others did not

history.modern_era: Since 2019 the movement has plateaued and partly fragmented, with academic study (Olshansky, Landrum, Asch, Paolillo) treating it as a paradigmatic case of online conspiracy ecology. Major figures include Mark Sargent (Enclosed World series, 2014), Eric Dubay, Robbie Davidson (FEIC organiser), and Patricia Steere.

video.name: Behind the Curve (2018)
video.description: Documentary by Daniel J. Clark documenting leading flat-Earth advocates, including the ring-laser-gyroscope experiment in which Bob Knodel's apparatus measured a 15° per hour rotation consistent with a spherical, rotating Earth.
video.analysis: Widely cited as a case study in confirmation bias; the gyroscope result is treated by physicists and skeptics as a self-administered refutation of the flat-Earth model.

documentary.name: Earth Not a Globe
documentary.analysis: Definitively refuted by Alfred Russel Wallace's 1870 repetition of the Bedford Level experiment and by every modern direct measurement of Earth's curvature.

documentary.name: Flat Earth Society
documentary.description: Online forum and journal of the modern (post-2009) Flat Earth Society.
documentary.analysis: Primary documentary corpus of the contemporary movement; treated by researchers (Olshansky 2020) as a key field site.

scientific.name: Sociological-conspiracy explanation
scientific.description: Researchers in science studies and communication treat flat-Earth belief as an emergent online conspiracy community sustained by recommendation algorithms, identity dynamics, and distrust of institutions, rather than by genuine empirical disagreement.
evidence_cited: Landrum & Olshansky surveys of FEIC attendees (2017–2018)
Three-faction model: faith-based, conspiracy-based, and experiential

skeptical.name: Direct empirical refutation
skeptical.description: Earth's spherical shape is supported by overlapping evidence from Eratosthenes (c. 240 BCE), the Bedford Level repetition by Wallace (1870), satellite imagery, GPS, gravitational physics, ship-disappearance over the horizon, lunar-eclipse geometry, and direct observation from orbit.
evidence_cited: Continuous satellite imagery since the late 1950s

skeptical.name: Self-administered refutation in Behind the Curve
skeptical.description: In the 2018 documentary Behind the Curve, the flat-Earth group's own ring-laser gyroscope detected a 15°/hour rotation — exactly Earth's spherical-rotation rate — which the experimenters then dismissed as instrument error.
evidence_cited: Self-built pendulum experiment in the same film

traditions: Modern online flat-Earth video, podcast, and conference culture
stories: Behind the Curve ring-laser gyroscope scene (2018)
folklore.beliefs: Modern flat-Earth belief combines biblical-literalist cosmology, distrust of NASA and government, and an empiricist self-image emphasising 'do your own research'. Researchers consistently identify it as a faith / identity community rather than a research programme.

literature: Earth Not a Globe — Samuel Rowbotham (1865)
Flat Earth: The History of an Infamous Idea — Christine Garwood (2007)
Inventing the Flat Earth — Jeffrey Burton Russell (1991)
film_tv: Behind the Curve (2018, dir. Daniel J. Clark)
Flat Earthers: To the Edge and Back (2023, BBC)
other: The Flat Earth Society online forums
'Globe Earth' rebuttal communities (Wolfie6020, SciManDan, Professor Dave Explains)

researchers.name: Christine Garwood
researchers.contribution: Flat Earth: The History of an Infamous Idea (2007), the standard scholarly history

researchers.name: Asheley R. Landrum
researchers.contribution: Empirical study of flat-Earth communities, including FEIC attendee surveys

researchers.name: Alex Olshansky
researchers.contribution: Co-author with Landrum on flat-Earth attitude and identity research

researchers.name: Kelly Weill
researchers.contribution: Off the Edge (2022); long-form journalism on the modern movement

researchers.name: Jeffrey Burton Russell
researchers.contribution: Inventing the Flat Earth (1991), debunking the myth that medieval Europeans believed in a flat Earth

organizations.name: The Flat Earth Society
organizations.focus: Online forum and journal of the contemporary movement

organizations.name: Center for Inquiry / Skeptical Inquirer
organizations.focus: Scientific skepticism, with extensive coverage of flat-Earth belief

research.ongoing_studies: Active research areas include the social-science of online conspiracy belief, platform-algorithm research on YouTube and TikTok, and journalistic field studies of FEIC and similar gatherings.

sources.title: Flat Earth: The History of an Infamous Idea
Inventing the Flat Earth: Columbus and Modern Historians
Off the Edge: Flat Earthers, Conspiracy Culture, and Why People Will Believe Anything
The role of identity and self-perception in flat-Earth beliefs
